title: Acromegaly
oneLiner: A chronic endocrine disorder of GH excess (usually from pituitary adenoma) causing progressive somatic enlargement and multisystem morbidity.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 45-year-old man presents to the orthopaedic clinic with bilateral knee pain. He has also noticed his ring no longer fits, his shoe size has gone up, and his wife says his voice sounds deeper. On examination you see a prominent jaw (prognathism), widely spaced teeth, large hands with spade-like fingers, coarse facial features, and oily skin. His blood pressure is 155/95 mmHg. He mentions he has been having more headaches recently and feels tired all the time.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Middle-aged adult (40-60) | Most common age at diagnosis – insidious onset over years |
| Ring no longer fits / shoe size increased | Soft tissue swelling and bony enlargement – pathognomonic for acromegaly |
| Deepening voice | Laryngeal hypertrophy and vocal cord thickening |
| Prognathism, widely spaced teeth | Mandibular overgrowth causing dental malocclusion |
| Spade-like fingers / large hands | Bony proliferation of phalanges and soft tissue hypertrophy |
| Coarse facial features, oily skin | GH/IGF-1 stimulation of sebaceous glands and dermal collagen |
| Hypertension | GH-induced sodium retention and increased systemic vascular resistance |
| Headaches, fatigue | Possible mass effect from pituitary adenoma (suprasellar extension) and/or metabolic effects |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Young adult with growth acceleration** — A 16-year-old with tall stature, large hands, and joint pain may be mistaken for familial tall stature or Marfan syndrome. Check IGF-1 if there are disproportionate features (e.g., jaw enlargement not seen in simple tall stature).
- **Incidental pituitary adenoma** — MRI for headache reveals a pituitary lesion, but the patient has no visible features because acromegaly is still mild. You must screen for GH excess because even subclinical acromegaly harms long-term outcomes.
- **Cardiomyopathy without classic features** — A patient with unexplained left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, and hypertension whose facial changes were dismissed as "normal aging." Always consider acromegaly if hypertrophic cardiomyopathy has an atypical pattern (eccentric hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction initially).

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:** Acromegaly is a chronic disease in which a benign tumour of the pituitary gland (almost always an adenoma) secretes too much growth hormone (GH). This excess GH forces the liver to produce high levels of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1), which drives abnormal growth of bones and soft tissues, especially in the hands, feet, and face. Unlike gigantism, which begins before epiphyseal fusion (childhood/teens), acromegaly starts in adulthood, so long bones don't lengthen but do widen.

**Why this topic matters clinically:**

- **Rarity that's often missed:** Incidence ~3-4 per million/year, yet diagnosis is delayed 7-10 years after symptom onset. This delay means irreversible complications are common (cardiovascular, respiratory, metabolic, joint disease).
- **High morbidity and mortality:** Untreated acromegaly reduces life expectancy by ~10 years (mainly from cardiovascular disease, respiratory failure, and colorectal cancer). Even with treatment, many comorbidities persist.
- **Critical endocrine emergency:** Pituitary apoplexy (haemorrhage into the adenoma) can present with sudden headache, vision loss, and adrenal crisis — you must recognise it and give IV hydrocortisone.
- **Every junior doctor will encounter it** — in orthopaedics (joint pain, carpal tunnel), cardiology (LVH, hypertension), diabetic clinic (insulin resistance), sleep clinic (OSA), or as an incidental MRI finding.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of disease development:**

1. A monoclonal pituitary somatotroph adenoma arises spontaneously (usually sporadic, rarely familial in MEN-1, Carney complex, or AIP mutations). The adenoma autonomously secretes GH without feedback suppression.
2. GH travels to the liver, where it stimulates production of IGF-1 (somatomedin C). IGF-1 is the main effector hormone that causes peripheral tissue overgrowth.
3. Elevated IGF-1 feeds back to the pituitary and hypothalamus, trying to suppress GH — but the adenoma is resistant, so GH stays high. The normal pulsatile GH pattern is lost.
4. High GH + IGF-1 drive:
   - **Skeletal growth:** periosteal bone deposition; mandible widens (prognathism), sinuses enlarge (frontal bossing), vertebral bodies expand (but not height), cartilage overgrowth (joint space widening).
   - **Soft tissue hypertrophy:** tongue, vocal cords, liver, kidneys, heart, skin (↑ collagen cross-linking, ↑ sebum).
   - **Metabolic:** GH opposes insulin action → insulin resistance → hyperinsulinaemia → glucose intolerance/diabetes. GH also increases renal sodium reabsorption → volume expansion → hypertension.
   - **Sleep:** tongue and pharyngeal soft tissue hypertrophy → obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA).
   - **Colon:** IGF-1 promotes epithelial proliferation → increased risk of colonic polyps and colorectal cancer.
   - **Cardiovascular:** GH/IGF-1 cause myocyte hypertrophy, interstitial fibrosis → concentric LVH → diastolic dysfunction → eventually systolic heart failure.

**Mechanism → Sign mapping:**

- GH excess → renal Na⁺ retention → plasma volume expansion → **hypertension** (often the earliest sign).
- IGF-1 → stimulation of cartilage and bone → **joint pain, osteoarthritis** (especially at knees, hips, spine).
- Laryngeal hypertrophy → **deep voice, stridor (rare)**, worse when supine.
- Macroglossia (large tongue) → **tooth spacing, dysarthria, sleep apnoea**.
- Carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve compression by hypertrophied transverse carpal ligament) → **hand paraesthesiae**.
- Hyperhidrosis (increased sweat gland activity) → **oily, sweaty skin**.
- Visceromegaly (heart, liver, kidneys) → **abdominal fullness, LVH on echo**.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The vignette's "ring no longer fits" = soft tissue hypertrophy + bony enlargement of digit. "Deep voice" = laryngeal hypertrophy. "Widely spaced teeth" = macroglossia pushes teeth apart + mandibular overgrowth. "Headaches" = pituitary adenoma mass effect (stretching diaphragma sellae) or hypertension.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Cause category | Specific cause | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Most common (>98%)** | GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (sporadic, usually macroadenoma >1 cm) | Classic acromegalic features + pituitary mass on MRI. No family history. |
| **Genetic syndromes** | MEN type 1 (MEN1 gene) | Acromegaly + hyperparathyroidism + pNET. Think "3 Ps": Pituitary, Parathyroid, Pancreas. |
| | Carney complex (PRKAR1A gene) | Acromegaly + skin myxomas + spotty pigmentation + testicular/thyroid tumours. |
| | Familial isolated pituitary adenoma (AIP gene) | Young age (≤30) at presentation. Often aggressive adenoma. Ask about family history of pituitary tumours. |
| **Very rare (<2%)** | Ectopic GHRH secretion | Carcinoid/pancreatic neuroendocrine tumour – causes pituitary somatotroph hyperplasia. Look for flushing, wheezing. Measure GHRH levels. |
| | Ectopic GH secretion | Lymphoma, often mediastinal. Truly exceptional. |

**Key point:** 99% of acromegaly is caused by a pituitary somatotroph adenoma. If the tumour is microadenoma (<1 cm), acromegaly is usually less severe and features may be subtle.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (Endocrine Society guidelines):**

- **Confirmed diagnosis:** Elevated IGF-1 (age- and sex-matched) **plus** failure to suppress GH to <1 ng/mL after 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) – "GH nadir >1 ng/mL".
- **Radiological confirmation:** Pituitary MRI (with gadolinium) → identifies adenoma, size, and relation to optic chiasm.

**Test-by-test breakdown:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Serum IGF-1** | Integrated measure of GH action over ~24 h (GH is pulsatile, so a random GH alone is unreliable) | First-line screening. High IGF-1 is strongly suggestive. | Must use age-adjusted lab ranges. Single elevated value → proceed to OGTT. Normal IGF-1 + symptoms → check repeat and OGTT. False positives: pregnancy, puberty, oral oestrogens (pills). |
| **Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)** | Assesses GH suppression. Normal: GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL. Acromegaly: GH nadir >1 ng/mL | Confirmatory test after elevated IGF-1. Also used post-treatment to assess cure. | Give 75 g glucose. Measure GH at 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min. **Key cutoff:** nadir GH >1 ng/mL = active disease. **Note:** Some labs use 0.4 ng/mL with ultrasensitive assays. |
| **Pituitary MRI** | Anatomy of sella turcica. Shows adenoma, size, invasion of cavernous sinus, chiasmal compression | Locate the tumour. Essential for surgical planning. | Microadenoma <1 cm (10% of acromegaly); macroadenoma ≥1 cm (90%). Empty sella with normal pituitary = not acromegaly. |
| **GH levels (random)** | Snap-shot GH (often high) | Not diagnostic alone; used for monitoring treatment response (pre- and post-surgery). | Avoid using single GH for diagnosis. However, a random GH >10 ng/mL in a non-pregnant adult without acromegaly is very suspicious. |
| **Other baseline labs** | Prolactin (elevated if tumour co-secretes prolactin or stalk effect), TFTs, cortisol, LH/FSH, testosterone | Rule out hyperprolactinaemia, hypopituitarism. | ↑ Prolactin may affect interpretation of GH assays (pregnancy false positive). Check cortisol to exclude hypoadrenalism. |
| **Colonoscopy** | Screening for colonic polyps/CRC | Because GH/IGF-1 increases colorectal cancer risk 2-3×. | Perform at diagnosis; repeat every 3-5 years if polyps found. |
| **Echocardiogram** | LVH, diastolic dysfunction, valve abnormalities | Cardiovascular complication is the main cause of death. | Baseline and annually, especially if hypertensive or symptoms develop. |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**

- **Rules in:** Elevated age-adjusted IGF-1 **plus** GH nadir >1 ng/mL on OGTT — diagnosis is confirmed. MRI showing pituitary adenoma seals the aetiology.
- **Rules out:** Normal IGF-1 for age (within reference range) essentially excludes active acromegaly. However, mild cases may have inconsistent IGF-1; if clinical suspicion persists, do OGTT.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) — *life-threatening presentation of pituitary apoplexy* only:**

- Suspect apoplexy: sudden severe headache, vomiting, visual field loss (bitemporal hemianopia or third nerve palsy), and hypotension/fever (adrenal crisis).
- **What to do:** Give STAT IV hydrocortisone 100 mg (then 50 mg Q4-6h) to avoid adrenal crisis. Arrange urgent neurosurgery consult. MRI without contrast (can be done if suspected). Monitor for diabetes insipidus.
- **Reasoning:** Pituitary apoplexy is a neuro-endocrine emergency. Adrenal insufficiency can kill before the tumour. Steroids buy time.

**Ward (24-72 h) — *once stable, non-emergent acromegaly management starts*:**

- **Surgery: Transsphenoidal adenomectomy** is first-line for most micro- and macro-adenomas (unless high surgical risk or unresectable). The goal: complete resection with remission.
- **Medical therapy – when needed:**
  - **Pre-operative** to reduce surgical risk (shrink tumour, improve cardiac/anaesthetic safety) – *only if delay to surgery*.
  - **Adjuvant** after surgery if not cured (persistent GH nadir >1 ng/mL or elevated IGF-1).
  - **Primary therapy** if surgery contraindicated (e.g., cavernous sinus invasion, elderly, frail).
- **First-line medical options:**
  - **Somatostatin analogues:** Octreotide LAR (long-acting release) 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks. Or lanreotide Autogel 60-120 mg SC every 4 weeks. They suppress GH secretion from the adenoma. **Common side effects:** gallstones (ultrasound baseline), bradycardia, hyperglycaemia (but can paradoxically improve glucose in some).
  - **GH receptor antagonist:** Pegvisomant 10-20 mg SC daily (blocks peripheral GH action, so IGF-1 falls). Useful if somatostatin analogues fail. **Monitor LFTs** (transaminitis).
  - **Dopamine agonists:** Cabergoline 0.5-3.5 mg twice weekly (only modestly effective, used for mild disease or combined with other agents).
- **Radiotherapy** (stereotactic or conventional) is reserved for aggressive residual or recurrent tumours. Effects take years.

**Long-term — *monitoring and comorbidity management*:**

- **Post-surgery:** Check IGF-1 and GH on OGTT at 3 months, then annually. Cure: IGF-1 normal, GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL (if assay sensitive) or <1.0 ng/mL.
- **Cardiovascular:** Strict BP control (target <130/80), statin if dyslipidaemia, echo every 1-2 years.
- **Diabetes/IGT:** Screen HbA1c annually. Metformin first-line. If severe, consider somatostatin analogues (which paradoxically worsen glucose tolerance – avoid if poor control).
- **Sleep apnoea:** Refer for CPAP if symptomatic (snoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity). Weight loss helps.
- **Carpal tunnel:** Surgical release if severe.
- **Surveillance:** Annual IGF-1 (if stable for 5 years, may space). Colonoscopy every 3-5 years after initial screening.
- **Pregnancy:** Well-controlled acromegaly is not a contraindication but requires careful monitoring – stop pegvisomant (unknown effects), use somatostatin analogues sparingly; GH/IGF-1 normally rise in pregnancy.

**What NOT to do:**

- Don't delay surgery for "medical control" if a microadenoma is accessible – surgery is the fastest path to remission.
- Don't rely on a single normal GH after surgery without checking OGTT (post-op GH can be low due to residual suppression).
- Don't forget to screen for colorectal cancer (often missed).
- Don't give bromocriptine instead of cabergoline (cabergoline is more effective and better tolerated).
- Don't assume LVH is "just hypertension" – acromegalic cardiomyopathy can progress independently.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acromegaly | Gigantism | Pseudoacromegaly (severe insulin resistance) | Paget's disease of bone | Familial tall stature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Age of onset | Adult | Childhood / before epiphyseal fusion | Adult (often with severe type 2 diabetes / lipodystrophy) | Usually >55 years | Childhood |
| Height | Normal or slight increase | Extreme tall stature | Normal | No change | Tall |
| Hand/foot enlargement | Spade-like, ring size ↑ | Proportional gigantism (all bones long) | Possible soft tissue hypertrophy (acanthosis nigricans) | Not characteristic | Normal but large |
| Facial features | Prognathism, coarse, macroglossia | Mild coarsening | Coarse due to acanthosis nigricans, but jaw not prominent | Frontal bossing (skull thickening), but no prognathism | Proportionate, no coarsening |
| Skin | Oily, sweaty, thick | Oily, thick | Acanthosis nigricans, skin tags | Normal | Normal |
| Lab results | ↑ IGF-1, ↑ GH, no suppression on OGTT | ↑ IGF-1, ↑ GH, no suppression | IGF-1 normal, GH may be slightly elevated (resistance), ↑ insulin, ↑ C-peptide | Alk Phos ↑ (bone fraction), calcium normal, PTH normal | Normal GH/IGF-1 |
| Imaging | Pituitary adenoma on MRI | Pituitary adenoma | No pituitary tumour; possible ovarian/adrenal cause of hyperandrogenism | Skull XR: thickened diploë, bone scan: hot spots | Normal pituitary |
| Key differentiator | GH nadir >1 ng/mL on OGTT | Onset <18 years, growth charts >98th centile | Absence of skeletal acromegalic changes, severe hyperinsulinaemia | Elevated alkaline phosphatase, lytic/sclerotic bone lesions on XR | Normal GH dynamics, family history |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Early detection prevents irreversible damage. If untreated or under-treated:**

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cardiovascular death** | CHF, arrhythmia (conduction defects, ventricular ectopy), myocardial infarction | Dyspnoea, oedema, LVH on echo, prolonged QT interval, hypertension | Aggressive BP control, echo monitoring, treat LVH with ACEi/ARB. Consider somatostatin analogues for cardiomyopathy. |
| **Obstructive sleep apnoea** | Snoring, daytime somnolence, morning headache, witnessed apnoeas | Large neck circumference (>17 inches men), macroglossia, retrognathia (mild), Epworth Sleepiness Scale >10 | Refer for polysomnography. CPAP is mainstay. Somatostatin analogues can shrink pharyngeal soft tissue over months. |
| **Type 2 diabetes** | Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (or none) | HbA1c ≥6.5%, fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL, insulin resistance | Metformin ± SGLT2i. Somatostatin analogues may worsen glucose – use cautiously. |
| **Colorectal cancer / polyps** | Often asymptomatic until advanced | Blood in stools, change in bowel habit, iron-deficiency anaemia | Colonoscopy at diagnosis, repeat every 3-5 years. Refer to GI if polyps. |
| **Arthropathy** | Joint pain, stiffness, crepitus | Bilateral knee/hip osteoarthritis, thickened joint spaces on XR, chondrocalcinosis | Analgesia, physio, joint replacement if severe. No disease-specific therapy reverses bone changes. |
| **Carpal tunnel syndrome** | Hand paraesthesiae, thenar wasting | Positive Phalen's test, Tinel's sign, nerve conduction study | Splinting, NSAIDs, surgical release if persistent. |
| **Hypertension** | Often asymptomatic, but contributes to LVH | BP >130/80 mmHg, orthostatic changes | Lifestyle + ACEi/ARB. Avoid beta-blockers if bradycardia (somatostatin analogues cause bradycardia). |
| **Visual field defects** | Bitemporal hemianopia, monocular field loss, third nerve palsy | Reduced visual acuity, disc pallor on fundoscopy | Urgent MRI + neuro-ophthalmology. Surgical decompression. |
| **Pituitary apoplexy** (see section 6) | Sudden severe headache, vomiting, hypotension, vision loss, altered consciousness | CT/MRI shows haemorrhage within pituitary. Low cortisol, hyponatraemia | IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, neurosurgery. |

**Late-presentation patterns (the "untreated" stem):** A 60-year-old with long-standing acromegaly (diagnosed too late) presents in heart failure with severe LVH and diastolic dysfunction, needing biventricular pacing. Or a patient with hip osteoarthritis that is advanced – hip replacement is complicated by increased perioperative risk due to upper airway obstruction (difficult intubation). Or colonoscopy reveals numerous adenomas — the missed acromegaly is uncovered only then.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"ACROMEGALY"** — Features to look for:
   - **A** – Acral enlargement (hands, feet)
   - **C** – Coarse facial features
   - **R** – Ring size increase
   - **O** – Osteoarthritis
   - **M** – Macroglossia, Mandibular overgrowth
   - **E** – Elevated GH/IGF-1
   - **G** – Glucose intolerance
   - **A** – Apnoea (sleep)
   - **L** – Large stature (in gigantism)
   - **Y** – Y-shaped spade fingers

2. **"SNORR"** — Clues that should prompt you to check IGF-1:
   - **S** – Shoe size increase
   - **N** – Not fits ring
   - **O** – Obstructive sleep apnoea
   - **R** – Ring size change + coarse features
   - **R** – Recurrent headaches

3. **"HANDS-ON"** — The systemic complications:
   - **H** – Heart (LVH, hypertension)
   - **A** – Airway (OSA, difficult intubation)
   - **N** – Neck (goitre may be present if MEN-1)
   - **D** – Diabetes
   - **S** – Spine (stenosis, arthropathy)
   - **O** – Oncology (colon polyps)
   - **N** – Neuropathy (carpal tunnel, radiculopathy)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues for F1s):**

- **"The patient whose shoe size went up after age 40"** – almost pathognomonic for acromegaly. Normal adult shoe size does not increase beyond growth.
- **"The patient who gets carpal tunnel in both hands at age 50 with no obvious cause"** – think acromegaly, hypothyroidism, amyloidosis, or pregnancy. Ask about ring fit.
- **"The patient with hypertension, difficult to control, plus osteoarthritis of the knees and a family history of pituitary tumours"** – think MEN-1.
- **"The patient with a deep voice who snorts like a pig when sleeping"** – macroglossia + OSA. Anesthetists note: difficult mask ventilation.
- **"If you see a pituitary tumour on MRI and the patient looks normal, still test IGF-1"** – up to 30% of pituitary incidentalomas are hormonally active.
- **"Post-operative cure is defined by IGF-1 normal and GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL (or <1.0 ng/mL with older assays)"** – never assume a random normal GH means cure.
- **"After successful surgery, sleep apnoea may partially reverse, but arthropathy and LVH may not"** – early diagnosis is key.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Missing diabetes insipidus** after transsphenoidal surgery – check fluid balance; if polyuria and hypernatraemia, test serum and urine osmolality.
- **Not checking colonoscopy** at diagnosis – it's guideline-mandated; a missed colon polyp could progress to cancer.
- **Using testosterone replacement in a male with hypogonadism before treating acromegaly** – GH stimulates sex hormone-binding globulin; when GH drops, SHBG falls and free testosterone may normalise. Replace only if persistently low.
- **Thinking that acromegaly means "only orthopaedic"** – cardiac cause of death is #1.
- **Forgetting to screen for MEN-1** if acromegaly presents with hypercalcemia, family history, or multiple tumours.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**

- **Suspected pituitary apoplexy:** Sudden severe headache, vomiting, vision loss, hypotension (NEWS2 ≥5), GCS drop. → Call on-call endocrine registrar, neurosurgery, and critical care.
- **Post-operative deterioration:** After transsphenoidal surgery:
  - GCS <13 or new neurological deficit (e.g., third nerve palsy, hemiparesis)
  - Visual field worsening
  - Polyuria >200 mL/h in 2 consecutive hours + serum Na⁺ >145 mmol/L (suspect diabetes insipidus)
  - Hypotension not responding to fluids (consider adrenal crisis)
- **Significant biochemical deterioration:** Rapid rise in GH/IGF-1 on treatment (tumour regrowth, medical failure). Unlikely acute but if accompanied by symptoms – refer.

**Specific thresholds for ICU / HDU referral:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **NEWS2** | ≥5 (or any single red: SpO₂ <91%, respiratory rate >30, HR >130, GCS <13) | Immediate review. Escalate to critical care outreach. |
| **Lactate** | >2 mmol/L (with hypotension, or rising trend) | Suspect sepsis (e.g., post-operative meningitis if CSF leak). Consider ICU for monitoring. |
| **GCS** | ≤13 (or drop by ≥2) | Alert neurosurgeon, brain imaging, possible apoplexy. |
| **Serum Na⁺** | >150 mmol/L (new hypernatraemia) or <125 mmol/L (severe hyponatraemia after surgery) | Endocrine review. Hypernatraemia with polyuria = DI. Hyponatraemia = SIADH (common after pituitary surgery) or adrenal crisis. |
| **Hypotension** | SBP <90 mmHg (despite 1L fluid bolus) + signs of shock | Bolus 100 mg IV hydrocortisone (empiric for adrenal insufficiency). Call ITU. |
| **Pituitary surgery complications** | CSF leak (clear discharge from nose, glucose positive), meningitis (fever, neck stiffness) | Neurosurgery and microbiology urgently. |
| **Cardiovascular** | New arrhythmia (e.g., atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, ventricular tachycardia), acute heart failure with pulmonary oedema | Call cardiology, echocardiogram. Treat as per local ACS/arrhythmia protocols. |

**Reminder:** If you are a junior doctor on call and a patient with known acromegaly becomes acutely unwell (especially with hypotension, altered consciousness, or visual symptoms), **give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat before lab results**. The mortality from unrecognized adrenal insufficiency is nearly 100%. Do not

rawMarkdown:
## Acromegaly

**One-liner:** A chronic endocrine disorder of GH excess (usually from pituitary adenoma) causing progressive somatic enlargement and multisystem morbidity.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 45-year-old man presents to the orthopaedic clinic with bilateral knee pain. He has also noticed his ring no longer fits, his shoe size has gone up, and his wife says his voice sounds deeper. On examination you see a prominent jaw (prognathism), widely spaced teeth, large hands with spade-like fingers, coarse facial features, and oily skin. His blood pressure is 155/95 mmHg. He mentions he has been having more headaches recently and feels tired all the time.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| Middle-aged adult (40-60) | Most common age at diagnosis – insidious onset over years |
| Ring no longer fits / shoe size increased | Soft tissue swelling and bony enlargement – pathognomonic for acromegaly |
| Deepening voice | Laryngeal hypertrophy and vocal cord thickening |
| Prognathism, widely spaced teeth | Mandibular overgrowth causing dental malocclusion |
| Spade-like fingers / large hands | Bony proliferation of phalanges and soft tissue hypertrophy |
| Coarse facial features, oily skin | GH/IGF-1 stimulation of sebaceous glands and dermal collagen |
| Hypertension | GH-induced sodium retention and increased systemic vascular resistance |
| Headaches, fatigue | Possible mass effect from pituitary adenoma (suprasellar extension) and/or metabolic effects |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Young adult with growth acceleration** — A 16-year-old with tall stature, large hands, and joint pain may be mistaken for familial tall stature or Marfan syndrome. Check IGF-1 if there are disproportionate features (e.g., jaw enlargement not seen in simple tall stature).
- **Incidental pituitary adenoma** — MRI for headache reveals a pituitary lesion, but the patient has no visible features because acromegaly is still mild. You must screen for GH excess because even subclinical acromegaly harms long-term outcomes.
- **Cardiomyopathy without classic features** — A patient with unexplained left ventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction, and hypertension whose facial changes were dismissed as "normal aging." Always consider acromegaly if hypertrophic cardiomyopathy has an atypical pattern (eccentric hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction initially).

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:** Acromegaly is a chronic disease in which a benign tumour of the pituitary gland (almost always an adenoma) secretes too much growth hormone (GH). This excess GH forces the liver to produce high levels of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1), which drives abnormal growth of bones and soft tissues, especially in the hands, feet, and face. Unlike gigantism, which begins before epiphyseal fusion (childhood/teens), acromegaly starts in adulthood, so long bones don't lengthen but do widen.

**Why this topic matters clinically:**

- **Rarity that's often missed:** Incidence ~3-4 per million/year, yet diagnosis is delayed 7-10 years after symptom onset. This delay means irreversible complications are common (cardiovascular, respiratory, metabolic, joint disease).
- **High morbidity and mortality:** Untreated acromegaly reduces life expectancy by ~10 years (mainly from cardiovascular disease, respiratory failure, and colorectal cancer). Even with treatment, many comorbidities persist.
- **Critical endocrine emergency:** Pituitary apoplexy (haemorrhage into the adenoma) can present with sudden headache, vision loss, and adrenal crisis — you must recognise it and give IV hydrocortisone.
- **Every junior doctor will encounter it** — in orthopaedics (joint pain, carpal tunnel), cardiology (LVH, hypertension), diabetic clinic (insulin resistance), sleep clinic (OSA), or as an incidental MRI finding.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of disease development:**

1. A monoclonal pituitary somatotroph adenoma arises spontaneously (usually sporadic, rarely familial in MEN-1, Carney complex, or AIP mutations). The adenoma autonomously secretes GH without feedback suppression.
2. GH travels to the liver, where it stimulates production of IGF-1 (somatomedin C). IGF-1 is the main effector hormone that causes peripheral tissue overgrowth.
3. Elevated IGF-1 feeds back to the pituitary and hypothalamus, trying to suppress GH — but the adenoma is resistant, so GH stays high. The normal pulsatile GH pattern is lost.
4. High GH + IGF-1 drive:
   - **Skeletal growth:** periosteal bone deposition; mandible widens (prognathism), sinuses enlarge (frontal bossing), vertebral bodies expand (but not height), cartilage overgrowth (joint space widening).
   - **Soft tissue hypertrophy:** tongue, vocal cords, liver, kidneys, heart, skin (↑ collagen cross-linking, ↑ sebum).
   - **Metabolic:** GH opposes insulin action → insulin resistance → hyperinsulinaemia → glucose intolerance/diabetes. GH also increases renal sodium reabsorption → volume expansion → hypertension.
   - **Sleep:** tongue and pharyngeal soft tissue hypertrophy → obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA).
   - **Colon:** IGF-1 promotes epithelial proliferation → increased risk of colonic polyps and colorectal cancer.
   - **Cardiovascular:** GH/IGF-1 cause myocyte hypertrophy, interstitial fibrosis → concentric LVH → diastolic dysfunction → eventually systolic heart failure.

**Mechanism → Sign mapping:**

- GH excess → renal Na⁺ retention → plasma volume expansion → **hypertension** (often the earliest sign).
- IGF-1 → stimulation of cartilage and bone → **joint pain, osteoarthritis** (especially at knees, hips, spine).
- Laryngeal hypertrophy → **deep voice, stridor (rare)**, worse when supine.
- Macroglossia (large tongue) → **tooth spacing, dysarthria, sleep apnoea**.
- Carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve compression by hypertrophied transverse carpal ligament) → **hand paraesthesiae**.
- Hyperhidrosis (increased sweat gland activity) → **oily, sweaty skin**.
- Visceromegaly (heart, liver, kidneys) → **abdominal fullness, LVH on echo**.

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The vignette's "ring no longer fits" = soft tissue hypertrophy + bony enlargement of digit. "Deep voice" = laryngeal hypertrophy. "Widely spaced teeth" = macroglossia pushes teeth apart + mandibular overgrowth. "Headaches" = pituitary adenoma mass effect (stretching diaphragma sellae) or hypertension.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Cause category | Specific cause | Stem clue |
|---|---|---|
| **Most common (>98%)** | GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (sporadic, usually macroadenoma >1 cm) | Classic acromegalic features + pituitary mass on MRI. No family history. |
| **Genetic syndromes** | MEN type 1 (MEN1 gene) | Acromegaly + hyperparathyroidism + pNET. Think "3 Ps": Pituitary, Parathyroid, Pancreas. |
| | Carney complex (PRKAR1A gene) | Acromegaly + skin myxomas + spotty pigmentation + testicular/thyroid tumours. |
| | Familial isolated pituitary adenoma (AIP gene) | Young age (≤30) at presentation. Often aggressive adenoma. Ask about family history of pituitary tumours. |
| **Very rare (<2%)** | Ectopic GHRH secretion | Carcinoid/pancreatic neuroendocrine tumour – causes pituitary somatotroph hyperplasia. Look for flushing, wheezing. Measure GHRH levels. |
| | Ectopic GH secretion | Lymphoma, often mediastinal. Truly exceptional. |

**Key point:** 99% of acromegaly is caused by a pituitary somatotroph adenoma. If the tumour is microadenoma (<1 cm), acromegaly is usually less severe and features may be subtle.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (Endocrine Society guidelines):**

- **Confirmed diagnosis:** Elevated IGF-1 (age- and sex-matched) **plus** failure to suppress GH to <1 ng/mL after 75 g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) – "GH nadir >1 ng/mL".
- **Radiological confirmation:** Pituitary MRI (with gadolinium) → identifies adenoma, size, and relation to optic chiasm.

**Test-by-test breakdown:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Serum IGF-1** | Integrated measure of GH action over ~24 h (GH is pulsatile, so a random GH alone is unreliable) | First-line screening. High IGF-1 is strongly suggestive. | Must use age-adjusted lab ranges. Single elevated value → proceed to OGTT. Normal IGF-1 + symptoms → check repeat and OGTT. False positives: pregnancy, puberty, oral oestrogens (pills). |
| **Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)** | Assesses GH suppression. Normal: GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL. Acromegaly: GH nadir >1 ng/mL | Confirmatory test after elevated IGF-1. Also used post-treatment to assess cure. | Give 75 g glucose. Measure GH at 0, 30, 60, 90, and 120 min. **Key cutoff:** nadir GH >1 ng/mL = active disease. **Note:** Some labs use 0.4 ng/mL with ultrasensitive assays. |
| **Pituitary MRI** | Anatomy of sella turcica. Shows adenoma, size, invasion of cavernous sinus, chiasmal compression | Locate the tumour. Essential for surgical planning. | Microadenoma <1 cm (10% of acromegaly); macroadenoma ≥1 cm (90%). Empty sella with normal pituitary = not acromegaly. |
| **GH levels (random)** | Snap-shot GH (often high) | Not diagnostic alone; used for monitoring treatment response (pre- and post-surgery). | Avoid using single GH for diagnosis. However, a random GH >10 ng/mL in a non-pregnant adult without acromegaly is very suspicious. |
| **Other baseline labs** | Prolactin (elevated if tumour co-secretes prolactin or stalk effect), TFTs, cortisol, LH/FSH, testosterone | Rule out hyperprolactinaemia, hypopituitarism. | ↑ Prolactin may affect interpretation of GH assays (pregnancy false positive). Check cortisol to exclude hypoadrenalism. |
| **Colonoscopy** | Screening for colonic polyps/CRC | Because GH/IGF-1 increases colorectal cancer risk 2-3×. | Perform at diagnosis; repeat every 3-5 years if polyps found. |
| **Echocardiogram** | LVH, diastolic dysfunction, valve abnormalities | Cardiovascular complication is the main cause of death. | Baseline and annually, especially if hypertensive or symptoms develop. |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms / rules out**

- **Rules in:** Elevated age-adjusted IGF-1 **plus** GH nadir >1 ng/mL on OGTT — diagnosis is confirmed. MRI showing pituitary adenoma seals the aetiology.
- **Rules out:** Normal IGF-1 for age (within reference range) essentially excludes active acromegaly. However, mild cases may have inconsistent IGF-1; if clinical suspicion persists, do OGTT.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) — *life-threatening presentation of pituitary apoplexy* only:**

- Suspect apoplexy: sudden severe headache, vomiting, visual field loss (bitemporal hemianopia or third nerve palsy), and hypotension/fever (adrenal crisis).
- **What to do:** Give STAT IV hydrocortisone 100 mg (then 50 mg Q4-6h) to avoid adrenal crisis. Arrange urgent neurosurgery consult. MRI without contrast (can be done if suspected). Monitor for diabetes insipidus.
- **Reasoning:** Pituitary apoplexy is a neuro-endocrine emergency. Adrenal insufficiency can kill before the tumour. Steroids buy time.

**Ward (24-72 h) — *once stable, non-emergent acromegaly management starts*:**

- **Surgery: Transsphenoidal adenomectomy** is first-line for most micro- and macro-adenomas (unless high surgical risk or unresectable). The goal: complete resection with remission.
- **Medical therapy – when needed:**
  - **Pre-operative** to reduce surgical risk (shrink tumour, improve cardiac/anaesthetic safety) – *only if delay to surgery*.
  - **Adjuvant** after surgery if not cured (persistent GH nadir >1 ng/mL or elevated IGF-1).
  - **Primary therapy** if surgery contraindicated (e.g., cavernous sinus invasion, elderly, frail).
- **First-line medical options:**
  - **Somatostatin analogues:** Octreotide LAR (long-acting release) 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks. Or lanreotide Autogel 60-120 mg SC every 4 weeks. They suppress GH secretion from the adenoma. **Common side effects:** gallstones (ultrasound baseline), bradycardia, hyperglycaemia (but can paradoxically improve glucose in some).
  - **GH receptor antagonist:** Pegvisomant 10-20 mg SC daily (blocks peripheral GH action, so IGF-1 falls). Useful if somatostatin analogues fail. **Monitor LFTs** (transaminitis).
  - **Dopamine agonists:** Cabergoline 0.5-3.5 mg twice weekly (only modestly effective, used for mild disease or combined with other agents).
- **Radiotherapy** (stereotactic or conventional) is reserved for aggressive residual or recurrent tumours. Effects take years.

**Long-term — *monitoring and comorbidity management*:**

- **Post-surgery:** Check IGF-1 and GH on OGTT at 3 months, then annually. Cure: IGF-1 normal, GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL (if assay sensitive) or <1.0 ng/mL.
- **Cardiovascular:** Strict BP control (target <130/80), statin if dyslipidaemia, echo every 1-2 years.
- **Diabetes/IGT:** Screen HbA1c annually. Metformin first-line. If severe, consider somatostatin analogues (which paradoxically worsen glucose tolerance – avoid if poor control).
- **Sleep apnoea:** Refer for CPAP if symptomatic (snoring, daytime sleepiness, obesity). Weight loss helps.
- **Carpal tunnel:** Surgical release if severe.
- **Surveillance:** Annual IGF-1 (if stable for 5 years, may space). Colonoscopy every 3-5 years after initial screening.
- **Pregnancy:** Well-controlled acromegaly is not a contraindication but requires careful monitoring – stop pegvisomant (unknown effects), use somatostatin analogues sparingly; GH/IGF-1 normally rise in pregnancy.

**What NOT to do:**

- Don't delay surgery for "medical control" if a microadenoma is accessible – surgery is the fastest path to remission.
- Don't rely on a single normal GH after surgery without checking OGTT (post-op GH can be low due to residual suppression).
- Don't forget to screen for colorectal cancer (often missed).
- Don't give bromocriptine instead of cabergoline (cabergoline is more effective and better tolerated).
- Don't assume LVH is "just hypertension" – acromegalic cardiomyopathy can progress independently.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acromegaly | Gigantism | Pseudoacromegaly (severe insulin resistance) | Paget's disease of bone | Familial tall stature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Age of onset | Adult | Childhood / before epiphyseal fusion | Adult (often with severe type 2 diabetes / lipodystrophy) | Usually >55 years | Childhood |
| Height | Normal or slight increase | Extreme tall stature | Normal | No change | Tall |
| Hand/foot enlargement | Spade-like, ring size ↑ | Proportional gigantism (all bones long) | Possible soft tissue hypertrophy (acanthosis nigricans) | Not characteristic | Normal but large |
| Facial features | Prognathism, coarse, macroglossia | Mild coarsening | Coarse due to acanthosis nigricans, but jaw not prominent | Frontal bossing (skull thickening), but no prognathism | Proportionate, no coarsening |
| Skin | Oily, sweaty, thick | Oily, thick | Acanthosis nigricans, skin tags | Normal | Normal |
| Lab results | ↑ IGF-1, ↑ GH, no suppression on OGTT | ↑ IGF-1, ↑ GH, no suppression | IGF-1 normal, GH may be slightly elevated (resistance), ↑ insulin, ↑ C-peptide | Alk Phos ↑ (bone fraction), calcium normal, PTH normal | Normal GH/IGF-1 |
| Imaging | Pituitary adenoma on MRI | Pituitary adenoma | No pituitary tumour; possible ovarian/adrenal cause of hyperandrogenism | Skull XR: thickened diploë, bone scan: hot spots | Normal pituitary |
| Key differentiator | GH nadir >1 ng/mL on OGTT | Onset <18 years, growth charts >98th centile | Absence of skeletal acromegalic changes, severe hyperinsulinaemia | Elevated alkaline phosphatase, lytic/sclerotic bone lesions on XR | Normal GH dynamics, family history |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Early detection prevents irreversible damage. If untreated or under-treated:**

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cardiovascular death** | CHF, arrhythmia (conduction defects, ventricular ectopy), myocardial infarction | Dyspnoea, oedema, LVH on echo, prolonged QT interval, hypertension | Aggressive BP control, echo monitoring, treat LVH with ACEi/ARB. Consider somatostatin analogues for cardiomyopathy. |
| **Obstructive sleep apnoea** | Snoring, daytime somnolence, morning headache, witnessed apnoeas | Large neck circumference (>17 inches men), macroglossia, retrognathia (mild), Epworth Sleepiness Scale >10 | Refer for polysomnography. CPAP is mainstay. Somatostatin analogues can shrink pharyngeal soft tissue over months. |
| **Type 2 diabetes** | Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss (or none) | HbA1c ≥6.5%, fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL, insulin resistance | Metformin ± SGLT2i. Somatostatin analogues may worsen glucose – use cautiously. |
| **Colorectal cancer / polyps** | Often asymptomatic until advanced | Blood in stools, change in bowel habit, iron-deficiency anaemia | Colonoscopy at diagnosis, repeat every 3-5 years. Refer to GI if polyps. |
| **Arthropathy** | Joint pain, stiffness, crepitus | Bilateral knee/hip osteoarthritis, thickened joint spaces on XR, chondrocalcinosis | Analgesia, physio, joint replacement if severe. No disease-specific therapy reverses bone changes. |
| **Carpal tunnel syndrome** | Hand paraesthesiae, thenar wasting | Positive Phalen's test, Tinel's sign, nerve conduction study | Splinting, NSAIDs, surgical release if persistent. |
| **Hypertension** | Often asymptomatic, but contributes to LVH | BP >130/80 mmHg, orthostatic changes | Lifestyle + ACEi/ARB. Avoid beta-blockers if bradycardia (somatostatin analogues cause bradycardia). |
| **Visual field defects** | Bitemporal hemianopia, monocular field loss, third nerve palsy | Reduced visual acuity, disc pallor on fundoscopy | Urgent MRI + neuro-ophthalmology. Surgical decompression. |
| **Pituitary apoplexy** (see section 6) | Sudden severe headache, vomiting, hypotension, vision loss, altered consciousness | CT/MRI shows haemorrhage within pituitary. Low cortisol, hyponatraemia | IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, neurosurgery. |

**Late-presentation patterns (the "untreated" stem):** A 60-year-old with long-standing acromegaly (diagnosed too late) presents in heart failure with severe LVH and diastolic dysfunction, needing biventricular pacing. Or a patient with hip osteoarthritis that is advanced – hip replacement is complicated by increased perioperative risk due to upper airway obstruction (difficult intubation). Or colonoscopy reveals numerous adenomas — the missed acromegaly is uncovered only then.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **"ACROMEGALY"** — Features to look for:
   - **A** – Acral enlargement (hands, feet)
   - **C** – Coarse facial features
   - **R** – Ring size increase
   - **O** – Osteoarthritis
   - **M** – Macroglossia, Mandibular overgrowth
   - **E** – Elevated GH/IGF-1
   - **G** – Glucose intolerance
   - **A** – Apnoea (sleep)
   - **L** – Large stature (in gigantism)
   - **Y** – Y-shaped spade fingers

2. **"SNORR"** — Clues that should prompt you to check IGF-1:
   - **S** – Shoe size increase
   - **N** – Not fits ring
   - **O** – Obstructive sleep apnoea
   - **R** – Ring size change + coarse features
   - **R** – Recurrent headaches

3. **"HANDS-ON"** — The systemic complications:
   - **H** – Heart (LVH, hypertension)
   - **A** – Airway (OSA, difficult intubation)
   - **N** – Neck (goitre may be present if MEN-1)
   - **D** – Diabetes
   - **S** – Spine (stenosis, arthropathy)
   - **O** – Oncology (colon polyps)
   - **N** – Neuropathy (carpal tunnel, radiculopathy)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues for F1s):**

- **"The patient whose shoe size went up after age 40"** – almost pathognomonic for acromegaly. Normal adult shoe size does not increase beyond growth.
- **"The patient who gets carpal tunnel in both hands at age 50 with no obvious cause"** – think acromegaly, hypothyroidism, amyloidosis, or pregnancy. Ask about ring fit.
- **"The patient with hypertension, difficult to control, plus osteoarthritis of the knees and a family history of pituitary tumours"** – think MEN-1.
- **"The patient with a deep voice who snorts like a pig when sleeping"** – macroglossia + OSA. Anesthetists note: difficult mask ventilation.
- **"If you see a pituitary tumour on MRI and the patient looks normal, still test IGF-1"** – up to 30% of pituitary incidentalomas are hormonally active.
- **"Post-operative cure is defined by IGF-1 normal and GH nadir <0.4 ng/mL (or <1.0 ng/mL with older assays)"** – never assume a random normal GH means cure.
- **"After successful surgery, sleep apnoea may partially reverse, but arthropathy and LVH may not"** – early diagnosis is key.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Missing diabetes insipidus** after transsphenoidal surgery – check fluid balance; if polyuria and hypernatraemia, test serum and urine osmolality.
- **Not checking colonoscopy** at diagnosis – it's guideline-mandated; a missed colon polyp could progress to cancer.
- **Using testosterone replacement in a male with hypogonadism before treating acromegaly** – GH stimulates sex hormone-binding globulin; when GH drops, SHBG falls and free testosterone may normalise. Replace only if persistently low.
- **Thinking that acromegaly means "only orthopaedic"** – cardiac cause of death is #1.
- **Forgetting to screen for MEN-1** if acromegaly presents with hypercalcemia, family history, or multiple tumours.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**

- **Suspected pituitary apoplexy:** Sudden severe headache, vomiting, vision loss, hypotension (NEWS2 ≥5), GCS drop. → Call on-call endocrine registrar, neurosurgery, and critical care.
- **Post-operative deterioration:** After transsphenoidal surgery:
  - GCS <13 or new neurological deficit (e.g., third nerve palsy, hemiparesis)
  - Visual field worsening
  - Polyuria >200 mL/h in 2 consecutive hours + serum Na⁺ >145 mmol/L (suspect diabetes insipidus)
  - Hypotension not responding to fluids (consider adrenal crisis)
- **Significant biochemical deterioration:** Rapid rise in GH/IGF-1 on treatment (tumour regrowth, medical failure). Unlikely acute but if accompanied by symptoms – refer.

**Specific thresholds for ICU / HDU referral:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|---|---|---|
| **NEWS2** | ≥5 (or any single red: SpO₂ <91%, respiratory rate >30, HR >130, GCS <13) | Immediate review. Escalate to critical care outreach. |
| **Lactate** | >2 mmol/L (with hypotension, or rising trend) | Suspect sepsis (e.g., post-operative meningitis if CSF leak). Consider ICU for monitoring. |
| **GCS** | ≤13 (or drop by ≥2) | Alert neurosurgeon, brain imaging, possible apoplexy. |
| **Serum Na⁺** | >150 mmol/L (new hypernatraemia) or <125 mmol/L (severe hyponatraemia after surgery) | Endocrine review. Hypernatraemia with polyuria = DI. Hyponatraemia = SIADH (common after pituitary surgery) or adrenal crisis. |
| **Hypotension** | SBP <90 mmHg (despite 1L fluid bolus) + signs of shock | Bolus 100 mg IV hydrocortisone (empiric for adrenal insufficiency). Call ITU. |
| **Pituitary surgery complications** | CSF leak (clear discharge from nose, glucose positive), meningitis (fever, neck stiffness) | Neurosurgery and microbiology urgently. |
| **Cardiovascular** | New arrhythmia (e.g., atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, ventricular tachycardia), acute heart failure with pulmonary oedema | Call cardiology, echocardiogram. Treat as per local ACS/arrhythmia protocols. |

**Reminder:** If you are a junior doctor on call and a patient with known acromegaly becomes acutely unwell (especially with hypotension, altered consciousness, or visual symptoms), **give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat before lab results**. The mortality from unrecognized adrenal insufficiency is nearly 100%. Do not
